next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Halima set the rolled bedding on the nearer cot and kept both hands on the bundle, letting her back unhook one knot at a time. Under her palms the iron frame gave a small answering tremor as another coach backed out of the bus stand below. The window glass rattled in its frame. The lodge room was above a tire shop and beside the stand, and the whole building kept shuddering under other people's departures, as if it were ready to climb down and leave with them. The plaster had once been painted a clean blue. Sweat, handprints, and years of damp had turned it the color of old dishwater. A fan with one bent blade churned the hot air without improving it.

The day had been all ferries, office benches, one clerk's face after another, Aminul's careful voice, Nura's silence stretched so tight it had begun to feel like a second strap cutting across the room. She had thought the waiting itself would save her. People on the mainland were always making them wait. A waiting day ate questions. A missed launch, a missing signature, a file sent upstairs and not returned, all of that could carry a person until evening without the person having to say the thing she meant not to say.

Now there was nowhere for the day to go. The office had shut. The last boat back had gone. Aminul was across the corridor because the cheaper rooms downstairs rented by the hour and he would not leave two women in those. That had been plain in the way he did not look at the desk clerk while he counted the notes out. He had only said, with that same office politeness, that he would knock if there w...
open_hooks_to_carry: Travel off the char changes scale and shows the institutional contempt surrounding displacement.
The records office demonstrates that bureaucratic violence can be cold rather than loud.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...The tin roof held the day's heat and gave it back slowly. From far off, toward the ghat, a ferry engine coughed and caught. Someone in the lane began reciting prayer in a hoarse voice, not from piety exactly but because routine felt like protection after a night when walls and weather had both gone loose. Halima sat for a moment with the ledger hidden again and the new copy hidden elsewhere, listening to the house settle around that division.

The injury had edges now. If Mofiz showed his hand too early, or if Aminul asked the right question before more weather arrived, the loss no longer lived only in Halima's mouth. It was thin protection, but it was the first she had had since dawn.

Then she called to Nura to start letting people collect what had survived. There would be questions. There would be faces measuring her while they took back their damp lives. She was not ready for accusation, and she had no interest in defense before she had something firmer to set on the mat.

When the first knock came on the post, Halima put her palm flat on the charpoy rope and felt how much water still lived inside the house. Behind her Nura had already begun repacking the market bag with the papers that had to travel dry. Then Halima stood, wiped her hand on her sari, and went to answer it thinking of east-side names, of the copy under the lamp, of the cleaner version lying inside Nura's school packet where a greedy hand would have to choose differently to reach it, and of the first ferry, because if Aminul was going to hear this from her he had to hear it before Mofiz named it for him.
state_deltas_required: Find an administrative route that can protect households whose blood records will never reconcile cleanly.
Halima learns that a household-occupancy affidavit route exists, but it will require public testimony that exposes private histories.
